speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three on March 24 of 1996 in Washington, DC. And we're interviewing Professor Alfred Steer. This is Bruce Einhorn, and we're conversing in English. Was there an airport at Nuremberg that you physically landed in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was-- it was paved with those perforated steel strips. Have you seen those things? In other words, a temporary army installation.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got off the plane, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Grand Hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that where you were billeted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, everybody was to begin with. It was a tremendous, grand hotel. No question about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- it was great. It had only been hit once in, in this incredible bombing that just flattened Nuremberg. And the-- and one portion was absolutely carved out. I think it was four floors high at that point. And one point was cracked down the middle. And they billeted me on the fourth floor in the back. [LAUGHS] And I had to climb up-- no elevator, of course-- climb up four stairs-- four flights and then go down this long hallway. And all of a sudden, it turned pitch black. And it was cold. And what they had done, they had put tarpaulins over this hole and put a plank down over the hole. [LAUGHS] And they told me, now, don't lose your balance, or you're going to fall three stories right down through the [INAUDIBLE]. And then, at the far end of that was my room. No heat, of course. And-- now, they, they kept us there for, for a few days. But that was our center. And downstairs they had an excellent restaurant and a nightclub. And Friday night was the only act in town. And I saw some of the best vaudeville I have ever seen because all-- all the vaudeville-- [LAUGHS] --teams in Germany had no place to go and no place to act, you see, except the army billets, and so forth, including the, the Nuremberg one. And I saw some really superb stuff there. Dostert, with his Colonel rank, got a house assigned to him out in a suburb. And we moved down into that house. It was in Erlenstegen. It was 14, Bulowstrasse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had he flown over with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was already there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Now, did you have a roommate in the hotel at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you share the house with anyone else other than Colonel Dostert?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were-- there were five of us in the house altogether. It was Dostert, myself, Van Sastro, who was an army captain-- no, lieutenant at that point. Uberall. You know Uberall, don't you? And-- and Sieg Roth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, for those who don't, who is Uberall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uberall was a Jewish guy, had been a Viennese, had left Vienna in time. I never did get him to tell me exactly how he did that. He lived in Paris for a time. He spoke excellent French. And he was a very able, valuable assistant. He became, became my assistant.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I always envied that son of bitch. The women loved him. Why? This little squirt, you know? I, I couldn't see why. They just sort of glommed on-- on Peter. Well, I thought I was much more glommable, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I-- I always envied-- and it's still true today. All these, all these women that I talk to downstairs, oh, you remember Peter Uberall? Yeah, sure I remember him. [LAUGHTER] And what about Mr. Roth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sieg was-- turned out to be my best friend in that whole-- in that whole outfit. He was a Jewish boy born and brought up in the streets of Manhattan. And he came over and had been in the group that liberated Germany to begin with. Wait a minute. He landed on the beaches of Normandy a corporal. He wound up in the streets of Prague a captain. Every promotion a battlefield promotion. You should have seen the goddamn decorations. He had rows and rows of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you all interpreters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Now, wait a minute. He walked in when Dostert and I were organizing this arrangement and setting it up. This guy just walks in out of nowhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Roth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roth. And says, could we use him? And I talked to him. [LAUGHS] Do you speak German? Do you speak Yiddish? You know anything about Yiddish? Yiddish is a, a dialect, a medieval dialect of Frankfurt am Main-- German-- with a number of Hebrew words and a few Slavic words put in, you see, with a very definite, sharp, distinctive accent. So I start talking to him. And it turns out he's talking to me in the most beautiful Yiddish, [? you see, ?] [? you ?] ever heard. And but he also-- his parents had come from Yugoslavia, Serbia. And he spoke Serb. Now, we had practically nobody among our-- what do I say-- storehouse of language experts-- who spoke Eastern European languages, there were quite-- And I said to the colonel, let's grab this guy. He's going to be useful. And he-- sure enough, he was. And he turned out to be a very able administrator. And we became fast friends and stayed friends after the war, to much-- much to my surprise. He died here in '78. And well, I'll have to tell you this. He, he married this gal, Sally. And for awhile, he lived on Long Island. We lived in Connecticut. And we would be-- we would meet occasionally downtown for dinner. Or I'll have them over for Thanksgiving. Or they'd have us over or something. And there was a cordial but rather distant thing. And so, at Christmastime, we'd always get a card from Sally. And all of a sudden there, at the end, she started giving these very pessimistic-- well, Sieg isn't well. There's something wrong with Sieg. And finally I heard it was cancer in his spine, in the base, base of his spine. And I remember turning to Jean and saying, well, this-- this is-- sounds like the end of friend, Sieg, but I hadn't seen him for quite some time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then all of a sudden, I got a phone call, and it was Sally is on the wire. And she said, Al, I'm calling you at Sieg's request. And I thought, oh? Sieg is in Walter Reed, and he's in very bad shape. And they're trying-- he's got this cancer. And they're trying to get him well enough to ship him home so he can die at home. Well, I said-- And Sieg wants to talk to you. Now, friend, I don't know. That bowled me over. Somebody is just about the step off, you know, into eternity. He wants to talk to me? So I made a mistake. When Sally hung up the phone-- she gave me the number to call. When Sally hung up, I called right back. I called. I should've waited.

speaker: interviewee
text: All I could think of to say at that point was, listen Sieg, you've been in some tough starts, and you're a tough character. You can lick this thing. Now, come on, grit your teeth and beat it. It was much too late for that kind of talk, much too late. And I think if I had-- if I'd thought a little bit, if I'd waited a little bit, I'd have realized that what he really wanted me to say probably was something about what he had done, and what he'd accomplished, and what he had achieved. But he's buried over here in Arlington. And he's buried only about a hundred yards from where my wife's parents are buried. And every time we go over there, we take flowers and leave one at Sieg's grave, too. I, I had a lot of like and respect for that guy. Quite a guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was working as an interpreter-- you, Dostert, the other gentlemen as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you all lived together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, did you receive any training in Nuremberg before getting to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next? You get billeted and then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got billeted. And then we started trying to make this thing work, try to make this system work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, why don't you tell us about the system and what it was you were supposed to be doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well. Well, it was a system put into use by the-- by IBM. And it had been shipped to Sao Paulo, Brazil sometime in the '30s for some international conference and hadn't got there in time. But then it had been stored for 10 or 12 years, something, in Sao Paulo, which is, you know, tropical. And the insulation on the wires had corroded. The electronics had rusted, and it was in very poor shape. And so we had to put this into work at-- in use at Nuremberg and try to bring it up to snuff, and so forth. It was quite a job. It was quite a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was the equipment intended for?

speaker: interviewee
text: This type of thing exactly, this type of thing exactly. In other words, a multi-lingual, simultaneous interpret-- you would sit down in a chair here. And you would have a pair of earphones hanging over the thing. You put the earphones on your head. There was a dial here with five numbers on it. And you knew in advance number one was the original speaker, number two was English, number three was French, number four was Russian or German, or whatever. And you would dial the language you wanted to listen to. And you would sit there, and you would hear the entire proceeding, see, through these earphones in the-- in the language you wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had this ever been done before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why you were going to try to use it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because of the necessity to shorten the proceeding. If, if you didn't do this-- the old-fashioned way required, let's say for a one-hour speech, then there would have to be a one-hour translation into French, an additional one-hour translation into German, an additional one-hour translation into Russian, making a total of four hours to do one hour's worth of business. This was simply impossibly complicated-- difficult. I was trying to get a shortcut. That-- that intolerable delay-- use this four-language system. Now, that meant that the interpreter had to sit there behind their little screen with earphones on, listening to the speaker in, let's say, German. Then he would hold a microphone in his hand. And he would interpret immediately into English. Now, I don't know if you know anything about German. But the Germans have a nasty habit of hanging their verbs at the end of a sentence. Well, you couldn't wait for that verb for the end of a sentence because, if you did, by the time you got it all out, the speaker would be halfway through the next sentence, all of which you would have missed. In other words, you had to anticipate what that speaker was going to say when he got to the end of that sentence. [LAUGHS] And another thing the Germans do that's very nasty, they put the 'nicht,' the negative, just in front of the verb at the end of the sentence. So you would-- you would anticipate the, the verb that the speaker is working towards, you get it right, and then he'd get there and he'd put in a 'nicht' [GERMAN] on it, you see. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We, we had one girl that was real clever about that. She would say, you think what I said is right? Just the opposite. And then she's going--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: She's going full speed ahead. Now, who had identified or discovered this IBM system?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dostert.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is he the one who told you about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he deserves the credit for-- I don't know whether he discovered it. But as far as I know, he was the-- he discovered it and brought it over. And he persuaded the higher-ups some way to make a bet on this system and get the money and the backing, and so forth. And so we did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you train with this system?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not part-- yes. Short-- very short. What we did, we, we, we-- the court was in session from 9:00 to 5:00. And when we would bring in a new interpreter from, let's say, Paris or wherever, we would take him to the courtroom after 5:00, after everybody had left. And we would set up a dummy session and try him out. Now, here's what's going to happen. You sit there, and so forth. Then we'd try and everything. Then we'd stop, say, now, you have to improve this or fix that or something like that, [? see. ?] So that was the only training we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, professor, you've talked about living with three other people who worked as interpreters. Were there just the four of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, five of us. Didn't I say five?

speaker: interviewer
text: Five. Were there just five interpreters at Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did-- how did you get the rest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Let me-- let me go-- how many interpreters did we have at Nuremberg? We had the two categories of interpreters. We had about 36 or 40 who worked in the courtroom, simultaneous interpreters. Then we had eight old-fashioned, consecutive interpreters whom we used-- we would send out-- for instance, they would get word, there's a prisoner out 70 miles north at a-- at a prison. It's the one I want to interview. So we'd give the lawyer this single interpreter to go interview that prisoner, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were-- instead of 36, there were about 40 or 43-- 42, 43 interpreters altogether. Now, they were billeted at various places around through the Nuremberg area. I can't tell you-- there was no one, one spot. Who selected these interpreters?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did.

speaker: interviewer
text: By what means?

speaker: interviewee
text: By listening to them and putting them through tests.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did you find them?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I-- I toured much of Europe looking for them. We went, first of all, to Geneva because Geneva had been the United Nations-- the League of Nations. We got a few from there, but very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: I finally-- I found that the best source was Holland because if you're-- if you're live in one of the small European countries-- let's suppose you want to become a engineer and you're a Dutchman. All right. You go through the university up to a certain level, but then when are-- where are you going to get a series of texts on strain gauges with high tensile steel in Dutch?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They don't exist. You have to know French, German, English. And their, their upper education has to include texts and materials and so forth in French, German, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how, when you went to Geneva or when you went to Holland, did you locate the right people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Geneva, I went to the United-- to the League of Nations office. It was still sort of a half-dead organization at that point. In Holland, there had been a special interpreters or translators core in the Dutch army. And they had been disbanded, but I found the former commanding officer. And he put-- he had some names and lists, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I found a very fine place was the Paris telephone exchange because [LAUGHS] you-- in Paris, who's at these various upper class hotels? They're Frenchmen, they're Britishers, they're Spanish, they're-- they're Russians, they're Italians and Swedes, and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of these women have to be able to deal with these requests and so put these calls through, [? and so. ?] And so I-- I got quite a few people from the Paris telephone exchange.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you recruit men and women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it matter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I-- did I-- did you hear me telling about, about Virginia Grey at the meeting [? the other night? ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I wanted you to tell us about Virginia Grey. I-- you mentioned her. But how did you-- who was Virginia Grey and how did you find her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm not sure. All of a sudden, I-- she turns up in-- in Nuremberg. Her name was, I think, originally von Schon, Virginia von Schon-- her-- German-American family, lived in Milwaukee. She was a very proper young lady. I found out later she taught Sunday school in her church back in Milwaukee. And she was a very personable young lady. Gosh, she was attractive. And she was very good as an interpreter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And anyway, at one point-- do you want me to tell her story? All right. At one point, we had on the witness stand a concentration camp guard who was subhuman. He was an animal. God, he was the most-- one of the most repulsive individuals-- a slime ball-- I have ever seen or heard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wasn't in the courtroom when he started to, to give testimony. Peter Uberall was and he-- we always had a monitor sitting with the interpreters, you see. And so I dropped in that particular morning. And as I dropped in-- there was a little side door behind the interpreters. You can see it in one of those pictures. And I slipped in this door. And Peter was shaking his head and pointing at Virginia. And some of the others were pointing at Virginia. Something was doing-- going wrong. So I took my split headphone-- we had split headphones. I put the original in one ear and the translation in the other ear so I could tell how the interpreter was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what language was the original?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what? The original was in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other one?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and she was trying to put it into English-- supposed to put it in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you heard both?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, I hear one in one ear. But that's no particular trick if you're used to it. Anyway, so, so he was-- he was-- he was interpreting. And what was happening was this. He would come--

speaker: interviewer
text: 'He,' meaning the witness?

speaker: interviewee
text: The witness would come to some place and he would say, now as far as the Jews, he was saying, you have to shit and piss and carry around all those goddamn Jews. She was saying-- she would-- there would be a moment delay. And then she would say, you just have to scorn the Jews. Whoa! That did-- she got the gist, but that is in no way an interpretation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would she-- would she translate any of the-- would she translate any of the profanity?

words.text: would she translate any of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Well, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, I-- obviously, I had-- this-- this couldn't go on, you see. And fortunately, it was right before the mid-morning break. At 10:30 every morning, there was a-- there was a 10-minute break so His His Lordship could have his cup of tea, you see. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- I-- I grabbed her, and I took her out in the hallway. And I said, Virginia this was-- got to stop. I said, you must translate exactly what you hear. She says, but commander, I don't use those words. I refuse to use those words. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, that's not the question. I said, the-- it's not a question that you, Virginia von Schon-- or I think she was calling herself Grey at that-- she got married. Anyway, that's not the point. The point is that you are a servant of the court. And the court is relying on you to give an accurate interpretation of what that man is saying so that they can get a firm judgment of what sort of a person he is. And you're not doing that. You're not living up to your obligation as an interpreter. Well, she wasn't about to accept that. I could see her clouding up to give me some kind of an argument. So I, I took the coward's way out. I ran out-- I walked away fast, you see. I got-- I got back into the court-- fortunately-- unfortunately just after the court started. I didn't get-- something held me up. I've forgotten what. And just as I slid into my seat, I heard this gust of laughter. Well, laughter, at that point-- absolutely impossible. It could not be, you see. And I knew that the people who were laughing, were-- many of them were listening in English, you see. [LAUGHS] So I put on my headphone. And I could hear this fellow, 'blasphemy, indecency, blasphemy, indecency,' you see, and then, on the Jews. Now what would happen was this. Virginia, in her nice, Midwestern, female voice would say everything up to the obscenities. Then she would hand her microphone to the guy sitting next to her, who was a Lieutenant Colonel McIntosh in the British army, very Scottish, ready to do French into English, but that wasn't being used at the moment-- but who understood German just as well as he understood French.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he would give, [LAUGHS] in his Scottish accent, all these filthy, blasphemous expletives and hand the microphone back to her. And so you would get this back and forth between attractive female Midwestern American and this base of Scottish. Well, of course, it's-- it's-- the unannounced alternation is funny. But [? it was ?] beside the point. Interpretation is supposed to be barely noticeable. It's not supposed to interfere with what's going on. And this had turned into-- it turned it into a sideshow. And this we couldn't have. So I had to take Virginia out and put another [LAUGHS] interpreter in. Now, you've told us, obviously, that, that these interpreters were servants of the court. Tell us what court you mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: The court was the International Military Tribunal, set up according to two different international treaties worked out in Paris before the end of the war, or just at the end of the war. The British, the Americans, the French, and the Russians got together in London and agreed that they were going to have an international trial and that it would be-- everybody would share in it. And there would be interpretation and so forth. They set up-- they defined the crimes, and so forth and so on. So that when, when we got there, they-- our, our task was to make this thing work. And we were able to do it, thank god.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know-- well, let me ask you, did you work for the court?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. I worked for the court, but I was paid by the United States Navy. In other words, the US government's various different organs loaned us to the tribunal for the course of the trial and paid us. We were-- we were still on the payroll.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the chief of the tribunal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lord Justice Sir Geoffrey Lawrence.

speaker: interviewer
text: From what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Britisher. Britisher. Britisher.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of man was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very fine guy. I liked him. I thought of all of the personnel involved, he was the most eminent, the most noteworthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the other judges?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had a Justice Biddle.

speaker: interviewer
text: From what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: From US. Don't you know the Philadelphia Biddles, [? Frank? ?] Oh, he was a Philadelphia Biddle. And then we-- then we had a Judge Parker from North Carolina, very Southern. There were-- there were, each-- each country had two, a main one and an alternate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Biddle the main one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Biddle was the main one, and Parker was the alternate. The-- the Russians had Nikitchenko and-- I forget the other one. We had no respect for them whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't really understand, I don't think, what a trial meant. The idea that the trial-- anybody came before a trial might be acquitted and go free was unheard of in the Russian jurisprudence, apparently, you know? What about the French judge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Falco was the-- was the man. He-- he was very much a stickler for small points. But he was respected. He was a-- he was a good jurist, he was a good jurist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Lord Lawrence have a backup?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] His name, I can't think what his name was. But anyway, he was a purist for, for pure English.

speaker: interviewer
text: The alternate?

speaker: interviewee
text: The backup-- one of these double names, Hoodington-Smythe or something, you know? Something like-- anyway. And as anything you cannot do it in a court like that is have everybody's English come up to the super Oxford Cambridge aristocratic levels-- I can't do it in one word.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this alternate British judge ever complain then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, frequently. And we'd just have to-- mostly we just ignored him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the other judges, how did they treat you and the-- and the staff of interpreters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite-- quite well, quite well. Talking about this alternate British judge-- I wish I could remember his name. We had Sprecher, Sprecher--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is Sprecher?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not Sprecher. Well, anyway, a US-type from New York-- Brooklyn, I think-- had, had, the, the, the Deutsche Stunde on the radio. Prior to the war, you know, they'd have the Deutsche Stunde and the Yiddish Stunde. And then they would have a-- a French hour and a, I don't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these language broadcasts in the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, we picked him up from that, you see. And his English was quite good. He was quite sharp. He was a-- He was a captain in the US Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was an interpreter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, an interpreter.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it had a definite Brooklyn accent. And His Lordship, His British Lordship objected to the British accent-- to the Brooklyn accent. Well, that's just too bad. When he's on, that's what you're gonna hear, sir. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Why don't we take a break and change tapes? And we'll talk some more about the court.
